title: Camera movement motivation (film-school / ASC practice)
sources_note: Distilled from M. David Mullen ASC notes (when/why to move), film-school motivated vs unmotivated movement, StudioBinder camera-movement storytelling guides.
core_principle: When and why the camera moves matters more than the hardware used to move it.

mullen_three_reasons.note: ASC teaching summary: three legitimate reasons to move (can combine).

dramatic.desc: Heighten tension, tighten to emotional expression, isolate, or release after a beat.

logical_action.desc: Follow important action so the audience stays oriented; keep subject readable in frame.

dimensional.desc: Parallax and perspective shift sell real depth/distance on a 2D screen (e.g. slight move looking down a drop).

motivation_classes.desc: Pan/tilt/track follows subject motion so the audience stays oriented.
Move discloses off-screen person, object, or space that changes meaning.
examples: dolly past foreground to new subject

motivation_classes.desc: Push-in/out or creep heightens inner state without literal follow action.
Move teaches geography of the set or relationship of bodies in space.
Handheld/POV approximates character nervous system or POV.
Parallax from lateral/dolly move sells 3D depth on a 2D screen (technical-aesthetic).
Whip pan, crash zoom, rapid track inject pace; use sparingly and intentionally.

rules: Every non-static move must declare one primary motivation_class in motivation text
Unmotivated flashy moves are allowed only as deliberate style with clear narrative payoff
House style: prefer micro_drift / slow_push / pan / lateral over static_hold
Static hold only if necessary (macro insert readability, explicit locked plate, brief force) — and still use angled camera, not pure eye_level
Do not move only because the catalog has many options
Prefer story-visible motivation language over pipeline jargon

angled_static_ok.note: If locked-off is required, still choose slight_high/slight_low/low/high/dutch — not pure eye_level
